sections.paragraph:
πήρα, ας, ἡ (Hom. et al.; Jdth 10:5; 13:10, 15; SibOr 6, 15 Joseph.; Tat. 25, 1) a leather pouch used by travelers, knapsack, traveler’s bag, which Jesus’ disciples were directed not to take w. them when they were sent out, since it was not absolutely necessary (s. on ὑπόδημα) Mt 10:10; Mk 6:8; Lk 9:3; 10:4; 22:35; cp. vs. 36. But perh. this instruction has in mind the more specialized mng. beggar’s bag (Diog. L. 6, 33; Gk. ins fr. Syria: BCH 21, 1897, 60; PGM 4, 2381; 2400. Cp. Const. Apost. 3, 6. Such a bag was part of a Cynic itinerant preacher’s equipment [PWendland, Kultur 84. Crates the Cynic wrote a piece titled Πήρα: HDiels, Poetae Philosophi 1902 Fgm. 4 p. 218. See Dio Chrys. 49 (66), 21; Lucian, Dial. Mort. 1, 3; Alciphron 3, 19, 5].—Acc. to Diog. L. 6, 13 Antisthenes the Cynic was the first to fold his cloak double [so he could sleep on it] and take a staff and πήρα with him; cp. 6, 22 of Diogenes.—Dssm., LO 87 [LAE 108ff]; SKrauss, Αγγελος I 1925, 96ff; KRengstorf, Jebamot 1929, 214f). οἱ μάγοι … ἐξέβαλον ἀπὸ τῆς πήρας αὐτῶν δῶρα GJs 21:3 (after Mt 2:11).—Such a bag was also used by shepherds (Ammon. Gramm. [I/II a.d.], diff. 112 πήρα … φέρουσιν οἱ ποιμένες; Longus 1, 13, 1; 3, 15, 3; Aesop, Fab. 31b H. [s. H-H. 24 II app.]; Babr. 86, 2; Jos., Ant. 6, 185 π. ποιμενική; s. the statue of the Good Shepherd in the Lateran) Hv 5:1; Hs 6, 2, 5; 9, 10, 5.—DELG. M-M. TW.

Hom Hom , VIII b.c.—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

SibOr SibOr = Oracula Sibyllina, II–III a.d.—List 5

Joseph Joseph This abbr. used when no specific texts are cited (s. Jos.); I a.d.—List 5

Tat Tat , II a.d.—List 5

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

perh. perh. = perhaps

mng. mng. = meaning(s)

Diog. L. Diog. L. = Diogenes Laertius, III a.d.—List 5

Gk. Gk. = Greek

ins Ins, ins = Inscription, Inschrift, inscription(s). Without a period, esp. in lists, as at the beginning of entries; the capitalized form is used in titles. In conjunction with literary works this abbr. refers to the title or description of contents.

  1. fr. = from

BCH BCH = Bulletin de correspondance hellénique—List 6

PGM PGM = Papyri Graecae Magicae—List 4

Crates Crates , letters attributed to the poet-philosopher C.; date uncertain—List 5

Fgm. Fgm. = fragment, fragmentary

Dio Chrys Dio Chrys , I–II a.d.—List 5

Lucian Lucian , II a.d.—List 5

Diogenes Diogenes = letters attributed to the philosopher D. of Sinope; date uncertain—List 5

Dssm. Dssm. = ADeissmann, various works—List 6

LO LO = Licht vom Osten—s. Dssm., LO in List 6

LAE LAE , s. Dssm. LO—List 6

GJs GJs = Gospel of James (Protevangelium Jacobi), II a.d.—List 1

Longus Longus , II ?a.d.—List 5

Aesop Aesop = a calque for a variety of lit. associated with the name of Aesop—List 5

  1. H. = FHort, s. W-H.—List 1, beg.

H H = Hermas; cited, unless otherwise noted, according to MWhittaker; II a.d.—List 1

app. app. = appendix, apparatus (when ref. to marginal text-critical information, esp. in N.)

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

Hv Hv = Visions

Hs Hs = Similitudes

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

TW TW = Theologisches Wörterbuch zum NT; tr. GBromiley, Theological Dictionary of the NT—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 811
